describes the history and [future of the universe](/wiki/Future_of_an_expanding_universe "Future of an expanding universe") according to [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") cosmology. The history begins 13.863 [billion years](/wiki/Billion_years "Billion years") ago when the universe was extremely dense and hot, before any matter formed. The earliest stage in physical models is a vast expansion of space in a tiny fraction of a microsecond, followed by a slower expansion and the creation of matter and antimatter particles. These particles almost completely annihilate each other leaving energy and a small remainder of matter in the form of a quarks-gluon plasma. As the expansion continues, this plasma condenses into a sea of protons and neutrons reacting with neutrinos. By the one second mark the temperature has dropped enough that neutrinos can no longer react; this enormous number of primordial neutrinos continue to stream across the universe to this day.

The **chronology of the universe** describes the history and [future of the universe](/wiki/Future_of_an_expanding_universe "Future of an expanding universe") according to [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") cosmology. The history begins 13.863 [billion years](/wiki/Billion_years "Billion years") ago when the universe was extremely dense and hot, before any matter formed. The earliest stage in physical models is a vast expansion of space in a tiny fraction of a microsecond, followed by a slower expansion and the creation of matter and antimatter particles. These particles almost completely annihilate each other leaving energy and a small remainder of matter in the form of a quarks-gluon plasma. As the expansion continues, this plasma condenses into a sea of protons and neutrons reacting with neutrinos. By the one second mark the temperature has dropped enough that neutrinos can no longer react; this enormous number of primordial neutrinos continue to stream across the universe to this day.

By 6s the expansion cooling allows electrons and positrons to annihilate, creating another pulse of energy. Then the nuclei of the first elements form, primarily hydrogen and helium with a bit of lithium. After rapid changes in the 3 minutes of expansion, the universe cools for thousands of years to reach a temperature where atoms are stable. An intense set of photons become free; they had been absorbed and emitted by the plasma but they interact comparatively less with the atoms. These photons are visible today as the background temperature of the universe.

The next phase of the universe involved the gradual gravitational collapse of hydrogen gas. Eventually the force of gravity compressed and heated the gas, igniting nuclear fusion to create the first stars. Collections of stars formed galaxies and the larger structures we see today.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

### Expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Expansion")]

Main article: [Expansion of the universe](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe")

The current [accepted model](/wiki/Lambda-CDM "Lambda-CDM") of the history of the universe is based on the concept of the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang"): the universe started hot and dense then expanded and cooled.
Different particles interact during each major stage in the expansion; as the universe expands the density falls and some particle interactions cease to be important. The character of the universe changes. Moreover, the rate of the expansion itself depends upon the nature of the existing particles, creating an interplay between cosmology and [particle physics](/wiki/Particle_physics "Particle physics").[[1]](#cite_note-KolbTurner-2018-1): 71

### Time

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Time")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Look-back_time_by_redshift.png/250px-Look-back_time_by_redshift.png)](/wiki/File:Look-back_time_by_redshift.png)

The [lookback time](/wiki/Cosmic_time "Cosmic time") of extragalactic observations by their cosmological redshift up to z=20.[[2]](#cite_note-Pilipenko-2)

Main article: [Cosmic time](/wiki/Cosmic_time "Cosmic time")

In cosmology, time and space are connected: space expands as time increases. Time at each point in space (for example a galaxy) can be uniquely defined in terms of an imaginary clock at that point. These clocks move with the point in space as the universe expands; they are synchronized to a single point in the distant past. Light from distant galaxies is emitted in the past then travels at the speed of light: knowledge about a distant galaxy is limited to one point in time called the [lookback time](/wiki/Lookback_time "Lookback time"). During the journey from a distant point, the universe continues to expand, stretching the wavelength of the light along the way, an effect called [cosmological redshift](/wiki/Cosmological_redshift "Cosmological redshift").[[3]](#cite_note-Peacock-1998-3): 72 The redshift can be measured by comparing incoming light to known spectroscopic lines and the resulting value can be related to the [comoving distance](/wiki/Comoving_distance "Comoving distance") to the emitter.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Consequently, experimental knowledge about the chronology of the universe is derived by observing distant light.[[3]](#cite_note-Peacock-1998-3): 89

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/History-of-the-Universe.jpg/500px-History-of-the-Universe.jpg)](/wiki/File:History-of-the-Universe.jpg)

The NASA diagram shows the history of the universe from inflation until the present.[[5]](#cite_note-NCH_1-5)

The chronology of the universe can be divided into five parts:[[6]](#cite_note-Turner-Precision-6): 4.1

* *Inflation,* the first era supported by experimental evidence, a period of exponential expansion that ends with the conversion of energy into particles,
* *Quark soup,* the initial particles cool and coalesce, [dark matter](/wiki/Dark_matter "Dark matter") forms,
* *Big bang nucleosynthesis,* combining nucleons create the cores of the first atoms,
* *Gravity builds cosmic structure,* reduced density allows matter to dominate over radiation for control of expansion, photons decouple to form the [cosmic background radiation](/wiki/Cosmic_background_radiation "Cosmic background radiation"), and gravitational attraction builds stars, galaxies, and clusters of galaxies.
* *Cosmic acceleration,* continued expansion allows [dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy") to overcome gravitational force, inhibiting larger structures.

With these large subsections are many events and transitions. Older models divided the chronology differently, using different terminology or emphasis.

## Inflation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Inflation")]

:   *Before c. 10−32 seconds after the Big Bang*

Main articles: [Inflation (cosmology)](/wiki/Inflation_(cosmology) "Inflation (cosmology)") and [Expansion of the universe](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe")

At this point of the very early universe, the universe is thought to have expanded by at least a factor of 1026 in time on the order of 10−36 seconds. All of the mass-energy in all of the galaxies currently visible started in a sphere with a radius around 4×10−29 m, then grew to a sphere with a radius around 0.9m by the end of inflation.[[13]](#cite_note-Ryden-2016-13): 202 This phase of the [cosmic expansion history](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe#Expansion_history "Expansion of the universe") is known as [inflation](/wiki/Inflation_(cosmology) "Inflation (cosmology)")[[15]](#cite_note-Cox-2017-15): 3 or sometimes as the **inflationary epoch**.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Inflation explains how today's universe has concentrations of matter, like galaxies and clusters of galaxies, rather than having [matter spatially uniform through the universe](/wiki/Homogeneity_(physics) "Homogeneity (physics)").[[3]](#cite_note-Peacock-1998-3): 324 Tiny quantum fluctuations in the universe, amplified by inflation, are believed to be the basis of large-scale structures that formed much later.[[17]](#cite_note-Durrer-2008-17): 2

The mechanism that drove inflation remains unknown, although many models have been put forward. In several of the more prominent models, it is thought to have been triggered by the [separation](/wiki/Phase_transition "Phase transition") of the strong and electroweak interactions which ended the grand unification epoch. One of the theoretical products of this phase transition was a scalar field called the [inflaton field](/wiki/Inflaton "Inflaton").[[18]](#cite_note-18) As this field settled into its lowest-energy state throughout the universe, it generated an enormous repulsive force that led to a rapid expansion of the universe.

The rapid expansion meant that any potential particles (or other "unwanted" artifacts, such as topological defects) remaining from the time before inflation were now distributed very thinly across the universe.

## Reheating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Reheating")]

It is not known exactly when the inflationary epoch ended, but it is thought to have been between 10−33 and 10−32 seconds after the Big Bang. The rapid expansion of space meant that any [elementary particles](/wiki/Elementary_particle "Elementary particle") remaining from the grand unification epoch were now distributed very thinly across the universe to the point where there is no physical temperature that can be associated with them.[[15]](#cite_note-Cox-2017-15) However, the large potential energy of the inflaton field was released at the end of the inflationary epoch, as the inflaton field decayed into other particles, known as [reheating](/wiki/Reheating "Reheating"). This heating effect led to the universe being repopulated with a dense, hot mixture of [Standard Model](/wiki/Standard_Model "Standard Model") particles.

After inflation ended, the universe continued to expand. A region the size of a melon at that time has since grown to be our entire [observable universe](/wiki/Observable_universe "Observable universe").[[15]](#cite_note-Cox-2017-15)

## Hot Big Bang

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hot Big Bang")]

The physical model for the chronology of the universe with strong observational and theoretical support is called the hot Big Bang model.[[7]](#cite_note-Baumann-2022-7): 71 The concept includes an early state of extreme temperature and density followed by [expansion of the universe](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe") continuing to this day. A high-precision version of the Big Bang model using conventional physics, known as [Lambda-CDM](/wiki/Lambda-CDM "Lambda-CDM"), agrees with a wide array of astrophysical observations.[[19]](#cite_note-Olive_Peacock2024-19) The concept is not extrapolated back to zero time.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Within the standard model of cosmology the initial state is set by a process called inflation. The relative timeline for the earliest phenomena is unclear. Speculation on processes occurring before inflation involves physics considered [outside of standard cosmology](#Early_universe).

## Baryogenesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Baryogenesis")]

Main article: [Baryogenesis](/wiki/Baryogenesis "Baryogenesis")

[Baryons](/wiki/Baryon "Baryon") are subatomic particles such as protons and neutrons that are composed of three [quarks](/wiki/Quark "Quark"). It would be expected that both baryons, and particles known as [antibaryons](/wiki/Antimatter "Antimatter") would have formed in equal numbers. However, almost no antibaryons are observed in nature. It is not clear how this came about. Any explanation for this phenomenon must allow the [Sakharov conditions](/wiki/Baryogenesis#Sakharov_conditions "Baryogenesis") related to baryogenesis to have been satisfied at some time after the end of [cosmological inflation](/wiki/Cosmological_inflation "Cosmological inflation"). Current particle physics suggests asymmetries under which these conditions would be met, but these asymmetries appear to be too small to account for the observed baryon-antibaryon asymmetry of the universe.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Electroweak phase transition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Electroweak phase transition")]

Main article: [Electroweak symmetry breaking](/wiki/Higgs_mechanism "Higgs mechanism")

:   *10−12 seconds after the Big Bang*

As the universe's temperature continued to fall below 159.5±1.5 [GeV/*k*B](/wiki/Electronvolt#Temperature "Electronvolt"), [electroweak symmetry breaking](/wiki/Higgs_mechanism "Higgs mechanism") happened.[[22]](#cite_note-22) So far as we know, it was the penultimate symmetry breaking event in the formation of the universe, the final one being [chiral symmetry breaking](/wiki/Chiral_symmetry_breaking "Chiral symmetry breaking") in the quark sector. This has two related effects:

1. Via the [Higgs mechanism](/wiki/Higgs_mechanism "Higgs mechanism"), all elementary particles interacting with the Higgs field became massive, having been massless at higher energy levels.
2. As a side-effect, the weak nuclear force and electromagnetic force, and their respective [bosons](/wiki/Boson "Boson") (the [W and Z bosons](/wiki/W_and_Z_bosons "W and Z bosons") and photon) began to manifest differently in the present universe. Before electroweak symmetry breaking, these bosons were all massless particles and interacted over long distances, but at this point the W and Z bosons abruptly became massive particles only interacting over distances smaller than the size of an atom, while the photon remained massless and remained a long-distance interaction.

After electroweak symmetry breaking, the fundamental interactions we know of—gravitation, electromagnetic, weak and strong interactions—all took their present forms, and fundamental particles had their expected masses, but the temperature of the universe was still too high to allow the stable formation of many of the particles we now see in the universe, so there were no protons or neutrons, and therefore no atoms, [atomic nuclei](/wiki/Atomic_nucleus "Atomic nucleus"), or molecules. (More precisely, any composite particles that formed by chance almost immediately broke up again due to the extreme energies.)

## Quantum chromodynamics phase transition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Quantum chromodynamics phase transition")]

:   *Between 10−12 seconds and 10−5 seconds after the Big Bang*

Main article: [Quark epoch](/wiki/Quark_epoch "Quark epoch")

After cosmic inflation ended, the universe was filled with a hot [quark–gluon plasma](/wiki/Quark%E2%80%93gluon_plasma "Quark–gluon plasma"), the remains of reheating. From this point onwards the physics of the early universe is much better understood, and the energies involved in the [quark epoch](/wiki/Quark_epoch "Quark epoch") are directly accessible in particle physics experiments and other detectors.[[15]](#cite_note-Cox-2017-15): 5

The [quark epoch](/wiki/Quark_epoch "Quark epoch") began approximately 10−12 seconds after the Big Bang. This was the period in the evolution of the early universe immediately after electroweak symmetry breaking when the fundamental interactions of gravitation, electromagnetism, the strong interaction and the weak interaction had taken their present forms, but the temperature of the universe was still too high to allow [quarks](/wiki/Quark "Quark") to bind together to form [hadrons](/wiki/Hadron "Hadron"). The quark epoch ended when the universe was about 10−5 seconds old; two non-equilibrium events must have occurred next, formation of baryons and of dark matter.[[6]](#cite_note-Turner-Precision-6): 23

## Neutrino decoupling and cosmic neutrino background (CνB)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Neutrino decoupling and cosmic neutrino background (CνB)")]

:   *Around 1 second after the Big Bang*

Main articles: [Neutrino decoupling](/wiki/Neutrino_decoupling "Neutrino decoupling") and [Cosmic neutrino background](/wiki/Cosmic_neutrino_background "Cosmic neutrino background")

At approximately 1 second after the Big Bang neutrinos decouple and begin travelling freely through space. As neutrinos rarely interact with matter, these neutrinos still exist today, analogous to the much later cosmic microwave background emitted during recombination, around 370,000 years after the Big Bang. The neutrinos from this event have a very low energy, around 10−10 times the amount of those observable with present-day direct detection.[[23]](#cite_note-forbes_neutrino-23) Even high-energy neutrinos are [notoriously difficult to detect](/wiki/Neutrino_detector "Neutrino detector"), so this cosmic neutrino background (CνB) may not be directly observed in detail for many years, if at all.[[23]](#cite_note-forbes_neutrino-23)

However, Big Bang cosmology makes many predictions about the CνB, and there is very strong indirect evidence that the CνB exists, both from [Big Bang nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Big_Bang_nucleosynthesis "Big Bang nucleosynthesis") predictions of the helium abundance, and from anisotropies in the cosmic microwave background (CMB). One of these predictions is that neutrinos will have left a subtle imprint on the CMB. It is well known that the CMB has irregularities. Some of the CMB fluctuations were roughly regularly spaced, because of the effect of [baryonic acoustic oscillations](/wiki/Baryon_acoustic_oscillations "Baryon acoustic oscillations"). In theory, the decoupled neutrinos should have had a very slight effect on the [phase](/wiki/Phase_(waves) "Phase (waves)") of the various CMB fluctuations.[[23]](#cite_note-forbes_neutrino-23)

In 2015, it was reported that such shifts had been detected in the CMB. Moreover, the fluctuations corresponded to neutrinos of almost exactly the temperature predicted by Big Bang theory (1.96±0.02 K compared to a prediction of 1.95 K), and exactly three types of neutrino, the same number of [neutrino flavors](/wiki/Neutrino#Neutrino_flavor "Neutrino") predicted by the Standard Model.[[23]](#cite_note-forbes_neutrino-23)

Cosmological models of this early time remain unsettled.
The Standard Model of particle physics is only tested up to temperatures of order 1017K (10 TeV) in particle colliders, such as the [Large Hadron Collider](/wiki/Large_Hadron_Collider "Large Hadron Collider"). Moreover, new physical phenomena not yet covered by the Standard Model could have been important before the time of [neutrino decoupling](/wiki/Neutrino_decoupling "Neutrino decoupling"), when the temperature of the universe was about 1010K (1 MeV).[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Electron-positron annihilation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Electron-positron annihilation")]

:   *Between 1 second and 10 seconds after the Big Bang*

Main article: [Lepton epoch](/wiki/Lepton_epoch "Lepton epoch")

The majority of hadrons and anti-hadrons annihilate each other leaving [leptons](/wiki/Lepton "Lepton") (such as the [electron](/wiki/Electron "Electron"), [muons](/wiki/Muon "Muon") and certain neutrinos) and antileptons, dominating the mass of the universe.
Initially leptons and antileptons are produced in pairs. About 10 seconds after the Big Bang the temperature of the universe falls to the point at which new lepton–antilepton pairs are no longer created and most remaining leptons and antileptons quickly annihilated each other, giving rise to pairs of high-energy photons, and leaving a small residue of non-annihilated leptons.[[25]](#cite_note-KauffmannLecture4-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Chaisson-26)[[27]](#cite_note-TimelineBB-27) After most leptons and antileptons are annihilated, most of the [mass–energy](/wiki/Mass%E2%80%93energy_equivalence "Mass–energy equivalence") in the universe is left in the form of photons.[[27]](#cite_note-TimelineBB-27)

## Nucleosynthesis of light elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Nucleosynthesis of light elements")]

:   *Between 3 minutes and 20 minutes after the Big Bang*[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-karki_2011-29)

Main article: [Big Bang nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Big_Bang_nucleosynthesis "Big Bang nucleosynthesis")

Between about 3 and 20 minutes after the Big Bang nuclear fusion reactions convert a 1:7 mixture of neutrons and protons in to a mix of protons, deuterium (a proton fused with a neutron), 3He, 4He, with trace amounts of 7Li and 7Be. These reactions end when the temperature falls below the 0.07MeV needed for nuclear fusion. The final mixture depends upon the reaction rates, the temperature, and the density of the components. The reaction rates can be measured in [nuclear physics](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics") laboratories while the temperature and densities can be calculated from models of the expansion of the universe.[[30]](#cite_note-Schramm-1998-30): 313

About 25% of the protons, and all[[29]](#cite_note-karki_2011-29) the neutrons fuse to form deuterium, a hydrogen isotope, and almost all of the deuterium quickly fuses to form helium-4.[[30]](#cite_note-Schramm-1998-30) Helium-4 has much higher binding energy than nuclei with 5 to 8 nucleons so only trace amounts of those nuclei are created.
Heavier nuclei produced in stars do not appear because they require the combination of [three Helium-4 nuclei](/wiki/Triple-alpha_process "Triple-alpha process") and the density of Helium-4 is too low for many three way collisions to occur before the expansion cools the universe below the fusion temperature.[[31]](#cite_note-Dodelson-2003-31): 65
Small amounts of [tritium](/wiki/Tritium "Tritium") (another hydrogen isotope) and [beryllium](/wiki/Isotopes_of_beryllium "Isotopes of beryllium")-7 and -8 are formed, but these are unstable and quickly decay.[[29]](#cite_note-karki_2011-29) A small amount of deuterium is left unfused.[[29]](#cite_note-karki_2011-29)[[31]](#cite_note-Dodelson-2003-31): 70

The amounts of each light element in the early universe can be estimated from old galaxies, and is strong evidence for the Big Bang.[[30]](#cite_note-Schramm-1998-30): 313 For example, the Big Bang should produce about 1 neutron for every 7 protons, allowing for 25% of all nucleons to be fused into helium-4 (2 protons and 2 neutrons out of every 16 nucleons), and this is the amount we find today, and far more than can be explained by production in stars.[[29]](#cite_note-karki_2011-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Schramm-1998-30): 305 Similarly, deuterium fuses extremely easily; any alternative explanation must also explain how conditions existed for deuterium to form, but also left some of that deuterium unfused and not immediately fused again into helium.[[29]](#cite_note-karki_2011-29) Any alternative must also explain the proportions of the various light elements and their isotopes. A few isotopes, such as lithium-7, were found to be present in amounts that differed from theory.[[32]](#cite_note-Snowmass21-32)

## Matter-radiation equality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Matter-radiation equality")]

:   *47,000 years after the Big Bang*

Main articles: [Matter-dominated era](/wiki/Scale_factor_(cosmology)#Matter-dominated_era "Scale factor (cosmology)") and [Structure formation](/wiki/Structure_formation "Structure formation")

Until now, the universe's large-scale dynamics and behavior have been determined mainly by radiation—meaning, those constituents that move relativistically (at or near the speed of light), such as photons and neutrinos. As the universe cools, from around 47,000 years (redshift *z* = 3600), the universe's large-scale behavior becomes dominated by matter instead.[[13]](#cite_note-Ryden-2016-13): 96 This occurs because the energy density of matter begins to exceed both the energy density of radiation and the vacuum energy density[[33]](#cite_note-33): 497 Around or shortly after 47,000 years, the densities of non-relativistic matter (atomic nuclei) and relativistic radiation (photons) become equal, the [Jeans length](/wiki/Jeans_instability#Jeans'_length "Jeans instability"), which determines the smallest structures that can form (due to competition between gravitational attraction and pressure effects), begins to fall and perturbations, instead of being wiped out by [free streaming](/wiki/Free_streaming "Free streaming") [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation"), can begin to grow in amplitude.

According to the [Lambda-CDM model](/wiki/Lambda-CDM_model "Lambda-CDM model"), by this stage, the matter in the universe is around 84.5% [cold dark matter](/wiki/Cold_dark_matter "Cold dark matter") and 15.5% "ordinary" matter. There is overwhelming evidence that [dark matter](/wiki/Dark_matter "Dark matter") exists and dominates the universe, but since the exact nature of dark matter is still not understood, the Big Bang theory does not presently cover any stages in its formation.

From this point on, and for several billion years to come, the presence of dark matter accelerates the [formation of structure](/wiki/Structure_formation "Structure formation") in the universe. In the early universe, dark matter gradually gathers in huge filaments under the effects of gravity, collapsing faster than ordinary (baryonic) matter because its collapse is not slowed by [radiation pressure](/wiki/Radiation_pressure "Radiation pressure"). This amplifies the tiny inhomogeneities (irregularities) in the density of the universe which were left by cosmic inflation. Over time, slightly denser regions become denser and slightly rarefied (emptier) regions become more rarefied. Ordinary matter eventually gathers together faster than it would otherwise do, because of the presence of these concentrations of dark matter.

The properties of dark matter that allow it to collapse quickly without radiation pressure also mean that it cannot *lose* energy by radiation. Losing energy is necessary for particles to collapse into dense structures beyond a certain point. Therefore, dark matter collapses into huge but diffuse filaments and haloes, and not into stars or planets. Ordinary matter, which *can* lose energy by radiation, forms dense objects and also [gas clouds](/wiki/Interstellar_cloud "Interstellar cloud") when it collapses.

### Recombination, photon decoupling, and the cosmic microwave background (CMB)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Recombination, photon decoupling, and the cosmic microwave background (CMB)")]

Main articles: [Recombination (cosmology)](/wiki/Recombination_(cosmology) "Recombination (cosmology)") and [decoupling (cosmology)](/wiki/Decoupling_(cosmology) "Decoupling (cosmology)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/WMAP_2012.png/250px-WMAP_2012.png)](/wiki/File:WMAP_2012.png)

9-year [WMAP](/wiki/WMAP "WMAP") image of the [cosmic microwave background](/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background "Cosmic microwave background") radiation (2012).[[34]](#cite_note-Space-20121221-34)[[35]](#cite_note-arXiv-20121220-35) The radiation is [isotropic](/wiki/Isotropy "Isotropy") to roughly one part in 100,000.[[36]](#cite_note-36): 291

About 370,000 years after the Big Bang, two connected events occurred: the ending of recombination and [photon decoupling](/wiki/Decoupling_(cosmology) "Decoupling (cosmology)"). Recombination describes the ionized particles combining to form the first neutral atoms, and decoupling refers to the photons released ("decoupled") as the newly formed atoms settle into more stable energy states.

Just before recombination, the [baryonic matter](/wiki/Baryonic_matter "Baryonic matter") in the universe was at a temperature where it formed a hot ionized plasma. Most of the photons in the universe interacted with electrons and protons, and could not travel significant distances without interacting with ionized particles. As a result, the universe was opaque or "foggy". Although there was light, it was not possible to see, nor can we observe that light through telescopes.

Starting around 18,000 years, the universe has cooled to a point where free electrons can combine with helium [nuclei](/wiki/Atomic_nucleus "Atomic nucleus") to form He+  
 atoms. After around 50,000 years, as the universe cools, its behavior begins to be dominated by matter rather than radiation.[[13]](#cite_note-Ryden-2016-13): 95 At around 100,000 years, after the neutral helium atoms form, [helium hydride](/wiki/Helium_hydride_ion "Helium hydride ion") is the first [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule").[[37]](#cite_note-hydride-37) Much later, [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and helium hydride react to form molecular hydrogen (H2), the fuel needed for the first [stars](/wiki/Star "Star").[[38]](#cite_note-38) At about 370,000 years, neutral hydrogen atoms finish forming ("recombination" of hydrogen ions and electrons),[[39]](#cite_note-39): 120 greatly reducing the [Thomson scattering](/wiki/Thomson_scattering "Thomson scattering") of photons.[[19]](#cite_note-Olive_Peacock2024-19): 22.4.1[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[13]](#cite_note-Ryden-2016-13): 155 No longer scattered by free electrons, the photons were "[decoupled](/wiki/Decoupling_(cosmology) "Decoupling (cosmology)")" from the earlier plasma and propagated freely. The majority of these photons still exist as the [cosmic microwave background](/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background "Cosmic microwave background") (CMB).[[19]](#cite_note-Olive_Peacock2024-19): 22.4.3 This is the oldest era of the universe that we can directly observe today.

Directly combining in a low energy state (ground state) is less efficient, so these hydrogen atoms generally form with the electrons still in a high-energy state, and once combined, the electrons quickly release energy in the form of one or more photons as they transition to a low energy state. This release of photons is known as photon decoupling. Some of these decoupled photons are captured by other hydrogen atoms, the remainder remain free. By the end of recombination, most of the protons in the universe have formed neutral atoms. This change from charged to neutral particles means that the [mean free path](/wiki/Mean_free_path "Mean free path") photons can travel before capture in effect becomes infinite, so any decoupled photons that have not been captured can travel freely over long distances (see [Thomson scattering](/wiki/Thomson_scattering "Thomson scattering")). The universe has become transparent to visible [light](/wiki/Light "Light"), [radio waves](/wiki/Radio_wave "Radio wave") and other [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") for the first time in its history.

|  |
| --- |
| The background of this box approximates the original [4000 K color](/wiki/Color_temperature "Color temperature") of the [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon") released during decoupling, before they became [redshifted](/wiki/Redshift "Redshift") to form the [cosmic microwave background](/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background "Cosmic microwave background"). The entire universe would have appeared as a brilliantly glowing fog of a color similar to this and a temperature of 4000 K, at the time. |

The photons released by these newly formed hydrogen atoms initially had a [temperature/energy of around ~ 4000 K](/wiki/Color_temperature "Color temperature"). This would have been visible to the eye as a pale yellow/orange tinted, or "soft", white color.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Over billions of years since decoupling, as the universe has expanded, the photons have been [red-shifted](/wiki/Red-shift "Red-shift") from visible light to radio waves (microwave radiation corresponding to a temperature of about 2.7 K). Red shifting describes the photons acquiring longer wavelengths and lower [frequencies](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") as the universe expanded over billions of years, so that they gradually changed from visible light to radio waves. These same photons can still be detected as radio waves today. They form the cosmic microwave background, and they provide crucial evidence of the early universe and how it developed.

Around the same time as recombination, existing [pressure waves](/wiki/Longitudinal_wave "Longitudinal wave") within the electron-baryon plasma—known as [baryon acoustic oscillations](/wiki/Baryon_acoustic_oscillations "Baryon acoustic oscillations")—became embedded in the distribution of matter as it condensed, giving rise to a very slight preference in distribution of large-scale objects. Therefore, the cosmic microwave background is a picture of the universe at the end of this epoch including the tiny fluctuations generated during inflation (see [9-year WMAP image](#9-year_WMAP_image)), and the spread of objects such as galaxies in the universe is an indication of the scale and size of the universe as it developed over time.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Gravity builds cosmic structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Gravity builds cosmic structure")]

:   *370 thousand to about 1 billion years after the Big Bang*[[43]](#cite_note-43)

See also: [Hydrogen line](/wiki/Hydrogen_line "Hydrogen line") and [List of the most distant astronomical objects](/wiki/List_of_the_most_distant_astronomical_objects "List of the most distant astronomical objects")

Even before recombination and [decoupling](/wiki/Decoupling_(cosmology) "Decoupling (cosmology)"), matter began to accumulate around clumps of dark matter.[[6]](#cite_note-Turner-Precision-6): 4.1 Clouds of hydrogen collapsed very slowly to form stars and [galaxies](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy").

[Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope")—[Ultra Deep Field](/wiki/Hubble_Ultra-Deep_Field "Hubble Ultra-Deep Field") galaxies to [Legacy Field](/wiki/Hubble_Legacy_Field "Hubble Legacy Field") zoom out (video 00:50; 2 May 2019)

### Dark Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Dark Ages")]

See also: [21 centimeter radiation](/wiki/Hydrogen_line "Hydrogen line")

After recombination and decoupling, the universe was transparent and had cooled enough to allow light to travel long distances, but there were no light-producing structures such as stars and galaxies. Stars and galaxies are formed when dense regions of gas form due to the action of gravity, and this takes a long time within a near-uniform density of gas and on the scale required, so it is estimated that stars did not exist for perhaps hundreds of millions of years after recombination.

This period, known as the Dark Ages, began at photon decoupling around 370,000 years after the Big Bang and ends over a long period of time called reionization.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
During the Dark Ages, the temperature of the universe cooled from some 4000 K to about 60 K (3727 °C to about −213 °C), and only two sources of photons existed: the photons released during recombination/decoupling (as neutral hydrogen atoms formed), which we can still detect today as the cosmic microwave background (CMB), and photons occasionally released by neutral hydrogen atoms, known as the [21 cm spin line of neutral hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen_line "Hydrogen line"). The hydrogen spin line is in the microwave range of frequencies, and within 3 million years,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the CMB photons had redshifted out of visible light to [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared"); from that time until the first stars, there were no visible light photons. Other than perhaps some rare statistical anomalies, the universe was truly dark.

The first generation of stars, known as [Population III stars](/wiki/Stellar_population#Population_III_stars "Stellar population"), formed within a few hundred million years after the Big Bang.[[45]](#cite_note-45) These stars were the first source of visible light in the universe after recombination. Structures may have begun to emerge from around 150 million years, and early galaxies emerged from around 180 to 700 million years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As they emerged, the Dark Ages gradually ended. Because this process was gradual, the Dark Ages only ended fully at around 1 billion years, as the universe took on its present appearance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/NASA-WMAP-first-stars.jpg/250px-NASA-WMAP-first-stars.jpg)](/wiki/File:NASA-WMAP-first-stars.jpg)

Artist's impression of the first stars, 400 million years after the Big Bang

#### Oldest observations of stars and galaxies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Oldest observations of stars and galaxies")]

Main articles: [Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope"), [James Webb Space Telescope](/wiki/James_Webb_Space_Telescope "James Webb Space Telescope"), and [List of the most distant astronomical objects](/wiki/List_of_the_most_distant_astronomical_objects "List of the most distant astronomical objects")

At present, the oldest observations of stars and galaxies are from shortly after the start of [reionization](/wiki/Reionization "Reionization"), with galaxies such as [GN-z11](/wiki/GN-z11 "GN-z11") ([Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope"), 2016) at about z≈11.1 (about 400 million years cosmic time).[[46]](#cite_note-yale20160303-46)[[47]](#cite_note-heic1604-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Hubble's successor, the [James Webb Space Telescope](/wiki/James_Webb_Space_Telescope "James Webb Space Telescope"), launched December 2021, is designed to detect objects up to 100 times fainter than Hubble, and much earlier in the history of the universe, back to [redshift](/wiki/Redshift "Redshift") z≈20 (about 180 million years [cosmic time](/wiki/Cosmic_time "Cosmic time")).[[50]](#cite_note-deepersky-50)[[51]](#cite_note-FAQ_scientists-51) This is believed to be earlier than the first galaxies, and around the era of the first stars.[[50]](#cite_note-deepersky-50)

There is also an [observational effort](/wiki/Low-Frequency_Array_(LOFAR) "Low-Frequency Array (LOFAR)") underway to detect the faint 21 cm spin line radiation, as it is in principle an even more powerful tool than the cosmic microwave background for studying the early universe.

### Earliest structures and stars emerge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Earliest structures and stars emerge")]

:   *Around 150 million to 1 billion years after the Big Bang*

See also: [Stellar formation](/wiki/Star_formation "Star formation"), [Dwarf galaxy](/wiki/Dwarf_galaxy "Dwarf galaxy"), [Baryon acoustic oscillations](/wiki/Baryon_acoustic_oscillations "Baryon acoustic oscillations"), [Large-scale structure](/wiki/Observable_universe#Large-scale_structure "Observable universe"), [Structure formation](/wiki/Structure_formation "Structure formation"), and [Stelliferous Era](/wiki/Future_of_an_expanding_universe#Stelliferous_Era "Future of an expanding universe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Hubble_ultra_deep_field.jpg/250px-Hubble_ultra_deep_field.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hubble_ultra_deep_field.jpg)

The [Hubble Ultra Deep Fields](/wiki/Hubble_Ultra-Deep_Field "Hubble Ultra-Deep Field") often feature galaxies that are examples of what the early Stelliferous Era was like.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Hubble_-_infant_galaxy.jpg/250px-Hubble_-_infant_galaxy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hubble_-_infant_galaxy.jpg)

Another Hubble image shows an infant galaxy forming nearby, which means this happened very recently on the cosmological timescale. This shows that new galaxy formation in the universe is still occurring.

The matter in the universe is around 84.5% cold dark matter and 15.5% "ordinary" matter. Since the start of the matter-dominated era, dark matter has gradually been gathering in huge spread-out (diffuse) filaments under the effects of gravity. Ordinary matter eventually gathers together faster than it would otherwise, because of the presence of these concentrations of dark matter. It is also slightly more dense at regular distances due to early [baryon acoustic oscillations](/wiki/Baryon_acoustic_oscillations "Baryon acoustic oscillations") (BAO) which became embedded into the distribution of matter when photons decoupled. Unlike dark matter, ordinary matter can lose energy by many routes, which means that as it collapses, it can lose the energy which would otherwise hold it apart, and collapse more quickly, and into denser forms. Ordinary matter gathers where dark matter is denser, and in those places it collapses into clouds of mainly hydrogen gas. The first stars and galaxies form from these clouds. Where numerous galaxies have formed, galaxy clusters and superclusters will eventually arise. Large [voids](/wiki/Void_(cosmology) "Void (cosmology)") with few stars will develop between them, marking where dark matter became less common.

The exact timings of the first stars, galaxies, [supermassive black holes](/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole "Supermassive black hole"), and quasars, and the start and end timings and progression of the period known as [reionization](/wiki/Reionization "Reionization"), are still being actively researched, with new findings published periodically. As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit): the earliest confirmed galaxies (for example [GN-z11](/wiki/GN-z11 "GN-z11")) date from around 380–400 million years, suggesting surprisingly fast gas cloud condensation and stellar birth rates; and observations of the [Lyman-alpha forest](/wiki/Lyman-alpha_forest "Lyman-alpha forest"), and of other changes to the light from ancient objects, allow the timing for reionization and its eventual end to be narrowed down.

Structure formation in the Big Bang model proceeds hierarchically, due to gravitational collapse, with smaller structures forming before larger ones. The earliest structures to form are the first stars (known as [Population III stars](/wiki/Population_III_star "Population III star")), dwarf galaxies, and quasars (which are thought to be bright, early [active galaxies](/wiki/Active_galactic_nucleus "Active galactic nucleus") containing a supermassive black hole surrounded by an inward-spiraling [accretion disk](/wiki/Accretion_disk "Accretion disk") of gas). Before this epoch, the evolution of the universe could be understood through linear cosmological [perturbation theory](/wiki/Perturbation_theory "Perturbation theory"): that is, all structures could be understood as small deviations from a perfect homogeneous universe. This is computationally relatively easy to study. At this point non-linear structures begin to form, and the [computational problem](/wiki/Computational_problem "Computational problem") becomes much more difficult, involving, for example, [*N*-body simulations](/wiki/N-body_simulation "N-body simulation") with billions of particles. The [Bolshoi cosmological simulation](/wiki/Bolshoi_cosmological_simulation "Bolshoi cosmological simulation") is a high precision simulation of this era.

These Population III stars are also responsible for turning the few light elements that were formed in the Big Bang (hydrogen, helium and small amounts of lithium) into many heavier elements. They can be huge as well as perhaps small—and non-metallic (no elements except hydrogen and helium). The larger stars have very short lifetimes compared to most Main Sequence stars we see today, so they commonly finish burning their hydrogen fuel and explode as [supernovae](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova") after mere millions of years, seeding the universe with heavier elements over repeated generations. They mark the start of the Stelliferous Era.

As yet, no Population III stars have been found, so the understanding of them is based on [computational models](/wiki/Computational_model "Computational model") of their formation and evolution. Fortunately, observations of the cosmic microwave background radiation can be used to date when star formation began in earnest. Analysis of such observations made by the *Planck* microwave space telescope in 2016 concluded that the first generation of stars may have formed from around 300 million years after the Big Bang.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Quasars provide some additional evidence of early structure formation. Their light shows evidence of elements such as carbon, [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium"), [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") and oxygen. This is evidence that by the time quasars formed, a massive phase of star formation had already taken place, including sufficient generations of Population III stars to give rise to these elements.

### Reionization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Reionization")]

See also: [Reionization](/wiki/Reionization "Reionization"), [Dwarf galaxy](/wiki/Dwarf_galaxy "Dwarf galaxy"), and [Quasar](/wiki/Quasar "Quasar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Conditions_During_the_Era_of_Reionization_%28Illustration%29_%282023-122%29.jpg/250px-Conditions_During_the_Era_of_Reionization_%28Illustration%29_%282023-122%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Conditions_During_the_Era_of_Reionization_(Illustration)_(2023-122).jpg)

Phases of the reionization

As the first stars, dwarf galaxies and quasars gradually form, the intense radiation they emit reionizes much of the surrounding universe; splitting the neutral hydrogen atoms back into a plasma of free electrons and protons for the first time since recombination and decoupling.

Reionization is evidenced from observations of quasars. Quasars are a form of active galaxy, and the most luminous objects observed in the universe. Electrons in neutral hydrogen have specific patterns of absorbing ultraviolet photons, related to electron energy levels and called the [Lyman series](/wiki/Lyman_series "Lyman series"). Ionized hydrogen does not have electron energy levels of this kind. Therefore, light travelling through ionized hydrogen and neutral hydrogen shows different absorption lines. Ionized hydrogen in the [intergalactic medium](/wiki/Warm%E2%80%93hot_intergalactic_medium "Warm–hot intergalactic medium") (particularly electrons) can scatter light through [Thomson scattering](/wiki/Thomson_scattering "Thomson scattering") as it did before recombination, but the expansion of the universe and clumping of gas into galaxies resulted in a concentration too low to make the universe fully opaque by the time of reionization. Because of the immense distance travelled by light (billions of light years) to reach Earth from structures existing during reionization, any absorption by neutral hydrogen is redshifted by various amounts, rather than by one specific amount, indicating when the absorption of then-ultraviolet light happened. These features make it possible to study the state of ionization at many different times in the past.

Reionization began as "bubbles" of ionized hydrogen which became larger over time until the entire intergalactic medium was ionized, when the absorption lines by neutral hydrogen become rare.[[53]](#cite_note-dijkstra-53) The absorption was due to the general state of the universe (the intergalactic medium) and not due to passing through galaxies or other dense areas.[[53]](#cite_note-dijkstra-53) Reionization might have started to happen as early as *z* = 16 (250 million years of cosmic time) and was mostly complete by around *z* = 9 or 10 (500 million years), with the remaining neutral hydrogen becoming fully ionized *z* = 5 or 6 (1 billion years), when [Gunn-Peterson troughs](/wiki/Gunn-Peterson_trough "Gunn-Peterson trough") that show the presence of large amounts of neutral hydrogen disappear. The intergalactic medium remains predominantly ionized to the present day, the exception being some remaining neutral hydrogen clouds, which cause [Lyman-alpha forests](/wiki/Lyman-alpha_forest "Lyman-alpha forest") to appear in spectra.

These observations have narrowed down the period of time during which reionization took place, but the source of the photons that caused reionization is still not completely certain. To ionize neutral hydrogen, an energy larger than 13.6 [eV](/wiki/Electronvolt "Electronvolt") is required, which corresponds to [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet") photons with a wavelength of 91.2 [nm](/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre") or shorter, implying that the sources must have produced significant amounts of ultraviolet and higher energy. Protons and electrons will recombine if energy is not continuously provided to keep them apart, which also sets limits on how numerous the sources were and their longevity.[[54]](#cite_note-qso_source1-54) With these constraints, it is expected that quasars and first generation stars and galaxies were the main sources of energy.[[55]](#cite_note-Barkana-Loeb2001-55) The current leading candidates from most to least significant are currently believed to be Population III stars (the earliest stars; possibly 70%),[[56]](#cite_note-popIII_sim-56)[[57]](#cite_note-qso_z-57) dwarf galaxies (very early small high-energy galaxies; possibly 30%),[[58]](#cite_note-Bouwens_LLG-58) and a contribution from quasars (a class of [active galactic nuclei](/wiki/Active_galactic_nucleus "Active galactic nucleus")).[[54]](#cite_note-qso_source1-54)[[59]](#cite_note-qso_source0-59)[[60]](#cite_note-qso_source2-60)

However, by this time, matter had become far more spread out due to the ongoing expansion of the universe. Although the neutral hydrogen atoms were again ionized, the plasma was much more thin and diffuse, and photons were much less likely to be scattered. Despite being reionized, the universe remained largely transparent during reionization due how sparse the intergalactic medium was. Reionization gradually ended as the intergalactic medium became virtually completely ionized, although some regions of neutral hydrogen do exist, creating Lyman-alpha forests.

In August 2023, images of black holes and related matter in the very early universe by the [James Webb Space Telescope](/wiki/James_Webb_Space_Telescope "James Webb Space Telescope") were reported and discussed.[[61]](#cite_note-QM-20230814-61)

### Galaxies, clusters and superclusters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Galaxies, clusters and superclusters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Large-scale_structure_of_light_distribution_in_the_universe.jpg/250px-Large-scale_structure_of_light_distribution_in_the_universe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Large-scale_structure_of_light_distribution_in_the_universe.jpg)

Computer simulated view of the large-scale structure of a part of the universe about 50 million light-years across[[62]](#cite_note-62)

See also: [Galaxy formation and evolution](/wiki/Galaxy_formation_and_evolution "Galaxy formation and evolution")

Matter continues to draw together under the influence of gravity, to form galaxies. The stars from this time period, known as [Population II stars](/wiki/Stellar_population#Population_II_stars "Stellar population"), are formed early on in this process, with more recent [Population I stars](/wiki/Stellar_population#Population_I_stars "Stellar population") formed later. Gravitational attraction also gradually pulls galaxies towards each other to form groups, [clusters](/wiki/Galaxy_cluster "Galaxy cluster") and [superclusters](/wiki/Supercluster "Supercluster"). [Hubble Ultra Deep Field](/wiki/Hubble_Ultra-Deep_Field "Hubble Ultra-Deep Field") observations has identified a number of small galaxies merging to form larger ones, at 800 million years of cosmic time (13 billion years ago).[[63]](#cite_note-63) (This age estimate is now believed to be slightly overstated).[[64]](#cite_note-shorter-64)

### Universe as it appears today

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Universe as it appears today")]

From 1 billion years, and for about 12.8 billion years, the universe has looked much as it does today and it will continue to appear very similar for many billions of years into the future. The [thin disk](/wiki/Thin_disk "Thin disk") of the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") began to form when the universe was about 5 billion years old or 9 ± 2 [Gya](/wiki/Billion_years "Billion years").[[65]](#cite_note-Soderblom-2010-65) The [Solar System](/wiki/Formation_and_evolution_of_the_Solar_System "Formation and evolution of the Solar System") formed at about 9.2 billion years (4.6 Gya);[[19]](#cite_note-Olive_Peacock2024-19): 22.2.3 the oldest organic matter consistent with [life processes](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_evolutionary_history_of_life "Timeline of the evolutionary history of life") dates back 4 billion years.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The thinning of matter over time reduces the ability of the matter to gravitationally decelerate the expansion of the universe; in contrast, [dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy") is a constant factor tending to accelerate the expansion of the universe. The universe's expansion passed an [inflection point](/wiki/Inflection_point "Inflection point") about five or six billion years ago when the universe entered the modern "dark-energy-dominated era" where the universe's expansion is now accelerating rather than decelerating. The present-day universe is quite well understood, but beyond about 100 billion years of cosmic time (about 86 billion years in the future), scientists are less sure which path the universe will take.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Dark energy-dominated era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Dark energy-dominated era")]

:   *From about 9.8 billion years after the Big Bang*

Main articles: [Dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy") and [Scale factor (cosmology)](/wiki/Scale_factor_(cosmology) "Scale factor (cosmology)")

From about 9.8 billion years of cosmic time,[[13]](#cite_note-Ryden-2016-13): 96 the universe's large-scale behavior is believed to have gradually changed for the third time in its history. Its behavior had originally been dominated by radiation (relativistic constituents such as photons and neutrinos) for the first 47,000 years, and since about 370,000 years of cosmic time, its behavior had been dominated by matter. During its matter-dominated era, the expansion of the universe had begun to slow down, as gravity reined in the initial outward expansion. But from about 9.8 billion years of cosmic time, observations show that the expansion of the universe slowly stops decelerating, and gradually begins to accelerate again, instead.

While the precise cause is not known, the observation is accepted as correct by the cosmologist community. By far the most accepted understanding is that this is due to an unknown form of energy which has been given the name "dark energy".[[68]](#cite_note-NYT-20170220-68)[[69]](#cite_note-peebles-69) "Dark" in this context means that it is not directly observed, but its existence can be deduced by examining the gravitational effect it has on the universe. Research is ongoing to understand this dark energy. Dark energy is now believed to be the single largest component of the universe, as it constitutes about 68.3% of the entire [mass–energy](/wiki/Mass%E2%80%93energy "Mass–energy") of the physical universe.

Dark energy is believed to act like a [cosmological constant](/wiki/Cosmological_constant "Cosmological constant")—a scalar field that exists throughout space. Unlike gravity, the effects of such a field do not diminish (or only diminish slowly) as the universe grows. While matter and gravity have a greater effect initially, their effect quickly diminishes as the universe continues to expand. Objects in the universe, which are initially seen to be moving apart as the universe expands, continue to move apart, but their outward motion gradually slows down. This slowing effect becomes smaller as the universe becomes more spread out. Eventually, the outward and repulsive effect of dark energy begins to dominate over the inward pull of gravity. Instead of slowing down and perhaps beginning to move inward under the influence of gravity, from about 9.8 billion years of cosmic time, the expansion of space starts to slowly accelerate *outward* at a gradually *increasing* rate.

## Beyond standard cosmology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Beyond standard cosmology")]

### Cosmogenesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Cosmogenesis")]

Main articles: [Cosmogenesis](/wiki/Cosmogenesis "Cosmogenesis") and [Origin of the universe](/wiki/Origin_of_the_universe "Origin of the universe")

Cosmological models extrapolated back to 10−43 seconds combined with [particle physics](/wiki/Particle_physics "Particle physics") models both with and beyond the [Standard Model](/wiki/Standard_Model "Standard Model") allow well-informed speculation on the character and properties of the early universe.[[1]](#cite_note-KolbTurner-2018-1): 1

#### Singularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Singularity")]

Main article: [Initial singularity](/wiki/Initial_singularity "Initial singularity")

Approaching infinite temperature, a scale factor of zero, or time at zero is known to be outside of our physical models. Speculating about an initial [gravitational singularity](/wiki/Gravitational_singularity "Gravitational singularity") is not sensible: the conditions are outside of the range of the theory.[[3]](#cite_note-Peacock-1998-3): 305

#### Planck epoch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Planck epoch")]

:   *Times within 10−43 seconds of the Big Bang*

See also: [Planck units § In cosmology](/wiki/Planck_units#In_cosmology "Planck units")

Since the standard model of cosmology predicts expansion of the universe from a very hot time in the distant past, it can be followed back to smaller and smaller scales. However, it cannot be followed back to zero space. Below distance known as a [Planck length](/wiki/Planck_length "Planck length"), the basis for the equations breaks down. The energy of particles in this time is so large that quantum effects take over from classical equations for gravity. The Planck time, 10−43 seconds, is therefore the beginning time for the Big Bang model of cosmology.[[3]](#cite_note-Peacock-1998-3): 274

### Grand unification epoch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Grand unification epoch")]

Main article: [Grand unification epoch](/wiki/Grand_unification_epoch "Grand unification epoch")

:   *Between 10−43 seconds and 10−36 seconds after the Big Bang*[[70]](#cite_note-Silk-2005-70)

After the Planck era, the universe could, in principle, be modeled by extensions of the [Standard Model](/wiki/Standard_Model "Standard Model") of [particle physics](/wiki/Particle_physics "Particle physics"), for example, those called [grand unified theories](/wiki/Grand_unified_theories "Grand unified theories"). Many such theories have proposed, but none have been successful in producing quantitative agreement with modern astrophysical observations. Nevertheless, the time between 10−43 and 10−36 seconds has been called the grand unification epoch.[[70]](#cite_note-Silk-2005-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Before the GUT epoch, the temperature of the universe exceeded 1015 GeV. As the universe expanded and cooled, it may have crossed a [cosmological phase transition](/wiki/Cosmological_phase_transition "Cosmological phase transition"), which may have resulted in the large ratio of matter to antimatter we observe today. This phase transition is a [thermodynamic](/wiki/Thermodynamic "Thermodynamic") effect similar to [condensation](/wiki/Condensation "Condensation") of a gas or [freezing](/wiki/Freezing "Freezing") of a liquid. While the transition in the GUT epoch is speculative, electroweak and quark-hadron transitions which happen later are supported by theoretical models with some successful predictions.[[3]](#cite_note-Peacock-1998-3): 305

### Electroweak epoch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Electroweak epoch")]

Main article: [Electroweak epoch](/wiki/Electroweak_epoch "Electroweak epoch")

:   *Starting anywhere between 10−22 and 10−15 seconds after the Big Bang, until 10−12 seconds after the Big Bang*

Sometime after inflation, the created particles went through [thermalization](/wiki/Big_Bang#Thermalization "Big Bang"), where mutual interactions lead to [thermal equilibrium](/wiki/Thermal_equilibrium "Thermal equilibrium").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Before the [electroweak symmetry breaking](/wiki/Electroweak_interaction "Electroweak interaction"), at a temperature of around 1015 K, approximately 10−15 seconds after the Big Bang, the electromagnetic and weak interaction [had not yet separated](/wiki/Electroweak_epoch "Electroweak epoch"), and the gauge bosons and fermions had not yet gained mass through the [Higgs mechanism](/wiki/Higgs_mechanism "Higgs mechanism"). This epoch ended with [electroweak symmetry breaking](/wiki/Electroweak_symmetry_breaking "Electroweak symmetry breaking"), potentially through a [phase transition](/wiki/Cosmological_phase_transitions "Cosmological phase transitions"). In some extensions of the [Standard Model](/wiki/Standard_Model "Standard Model") of particle physics, [baryogenesis](/wiki/Baryogenesis "Baryogenesis") also happened at this stage, creating an imbalance between matter and antimatter (though in extensions to this model, this may have happened earlier). Little is known about the details of these processes.

### Far future and ultimate fate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chronology_of_the_universe&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Far future and ultimate fate")]

Main articles: [Ultimate fate of the universe](/wiki/Ultimate_fate_of_the_universe "Ultimate fate of the universe") and [Timeline of the far future](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_far_future "Timeline of the far future")

Further information: [Future of an expanding universe](/wiki/Future_of_an_expanding_universe "Future of an expanding universe") and [Heat death of the universe](/wiki/Heat_death_of_the_universe "Heat death of the universe")

There are several competing scenarios for the long-term evolution of the universe. Which of them will happen, if any, depends on the precise values of [physical constants](/wiki/Physical_constant "Physical constant") such as the cosmological constant, the possibility of [proton decay](/wiki/Proton_decay "Proton decay"), the [energy of the vacuum](/wiki/False_vacuum "False vacuum") (meaning, the energy of ["empty" space](/wiki/Quantum_vacuum_state "Quantum vacuum state") itself), and the natural laws [beyond the Standard Model](/wiki/Physics_beyond_the_Standard_Model "Physics beyond the Standard Model").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

If the expansion of the universe continues and it stays in its present form, eventually all but the nearest galaxies will be carried away from us by the expansion of space at such a velocity that the observable universe will be limited to [our own](/wiki/Laniakea_Supercluster "Laniakea Supercluster") gravitationally bound local [galaxy cluster](/wiki/Galaxy_cluster "Galaxy cluster"). In the very long term (after many trillions—thousands of billions—of years, cosmic time), the Stelliferous Era will end, as stars cease to be born and even the [longest-lived stars](/wiki/Red_dwarf "Red dwarf") gradually die. Beyond this, all objects in the universe will cool and (with the [possible exception of protons](/wiki/Proton_decay "Proton decay")) gradually decompose back to their constituent particles and then into subatomic particles and very low-level photons and other [fundamental particles](/wiki/Elementary_particle "Elementary particle"), by a variety of possible processes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The following scenarios have been proposed for the ultimate fate of the universe:

| Scenario | | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **[Heat death](/wiki/Heat_death_of_the_universe "Heat death of the universe")** | As expansion continues, the universe becomes larger, colder, and more dilute; in time, all structures eventually decompose to subatomic particles and photons. | In the case of indefinitely continuing cosmic expansion, the energy density in the universe will decrease until, after an estimated time of 101000 years, it reaches [thermodynamic equilibrium](/wiki/Thermodynamic_equilibrium "Thermodynamic equilibrium") and no more structure will be possible. This will happen only after an extremely long time because first, some (less than 0.1%)[[72]](#cite_note-72) matter will collapse into [black holes](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole"), which will then evaporate extremely slowly via [Hawking radiation](/wiki/Hawking_radiation "Hawking radiation"). The universe in this scenario will cease to be able to support life much earlier than this, after some 1014 years or so, when star formation ceases.[[14]](#cite_note-dying-14), §IID In some [Grand Unified Theories](/wiki/Grand_Unified_Theory "Grand Unified Theory"), proton decay after at least 1034 years will convert the remaining interstellar gas and stellar remnants into leptons (such as positrons and electrons) and photons. Some positrons and electrons will then recombine into photons.[[14]](#cite_note-dying-14), §IV, §VF In this case, the universe has reached a high-[entropy](/wiki/Entropy "Entropy") state consisting of a bath of particles and low-energy radiation. It is not known, however, whether it eventually achieves [thermodynamic equilibrium](/wiki/Thermodynamic_equilibrium "Thermodynamic equilibrium").[[14]](#cite_note-dying-14), §VIB, VID The hypothesis of a universal heat death stems from the 1850s ideas of [William Thomson](/wiki/William_Thomson,_1st_Baron_Kelvin "William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin") (Lord Kelvin), who extrapolated the classical theory of heat and irreversibility (as embodied in the first two laws of thermodynamics) to the universe as a whole.[[73]](#cite_note-73) |
| **[Big Rip](/wiki/Big_Rip "Big Rip")** | Expansion of space accelerates and at some point becomes so extreme that even subatomic particles and the fabric of [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime") are pulled apart and unable to exist. | For any value of the dark energy content of the universe where the negative pressure ratio is less than −1, the expansion rate of the universe will continue to increase without limit. Gravitationally bound systems, such as clusters of galaxies, galaxies, and ultimately the Solar System will be torn apart. Eventually the expansion will be so rapid as to overcome the electromagnetic forces holding molecules and atoms together. Even atomic nuclei will be torn apart. Finally, forces and interactions even on the [Planck scale](/wiki/Planck_units#Planck_scale "Planck units")—the smallest size for which the notion of "space" currently has a meaning—will no longer be able to occur as the fabric of spacetime itself is pulled apart and the universe as we know it will end in an unusual kind of singularity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] |
| **[Big Crunch](/wiki/Big_Crunch "Big Crunch")** | Expansion eventually slows and halts, then reverses as all matter accelerates towards its common centre. Currently considered to be likely incorrect. | In the opposite of the "Big Rip" scenario, the expansion of the universe would at some point be reversed and the universe would contract towards a hot, dense state. This is a required element of oscillatory universe scenarios, such as the [cyclic model](/wiki/Cyclic_model "Cyclic model"), although a Big Crunch does not necessarily imply an oscillatory universe. Current observations suggest that this model of the universe is unlikely to be correct, and the expansion will continue or even accelerate.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] |
| **[Vacuum instability](/wiki/False_vacuum "False vacuum")** | Collapse of the [quantum fields](/wiki/Quantum_field_theory "Quantum field theory") that underpin all forces, particles and structures, to a different form. | Cosmology traditionally has assumed a stable or at least [metastable](/wiki/Metastability "Metastability") universe, but the possibility of a [false vacuum](/wiki/False_vacuum "False vacuum") in [quantum field theory](/wiki/Quantum_field_theory "Quantum field theory") implies that the universe at any point in spacetime might spontaneously collapse into a lower-energy state (see [Bubble nucleation](/wiki/False_vacuum#Bubble_nucleation "False vacuum")), a more stable or "true vacuum", which would then expand outward from that point with the speed of light.[[74]](#cite_note-turnerwilczek-74) |

In this kind of protracted timescale, extremely rare [quantum phenomena](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") may also occur that are unlikely to be seen on a timescale smaller than trillions of years. These may also lead to unpredictable changes to the state of the universe which would not be likely to be significant on any smaller timescale. For example, on a timescale of millions of trillions of years, black holes might appear to evaporate almost instantly, uncommon [quantum tunnelling](/wiki/Quantum_tunnelling "Quantum tunnelling") phenomena would appear to be common, and quantum (or other) phenomena so unlikely that they might occur just once in a trillion years may occur many times.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
